transcript.text: Hey, Andi.
How are you?
I'm doing well.
And you?
I'm good.
Nice to meet you.
Nice to meet you, too.
Where are you calling from?
I'm in Greece right now.
Oh, lovely.
And you?
I'm on the east coast of the U.S., where it also feels like it's nighttime, because it's a very dreary day, so I didn't know if you were actually in the dark, or just your lighting.
It's 7 p.m.
here.
Yeah, yeah.
Cool.
Well, thank you for making the time, so late in the afternoon, or evening, I guess.
Great to connect.
I know we have 30 minutes.
originally had 45, so appreciate you being a little bit flexible there.
I can give you a bit of a background on how I'm thinking about the role.
I know you've already connected with Tucker, and then we'll spend most of the time talking about you, and I'll leave at least five minutes at the end for you to ask me any extra questions.
So the account directors at GrowthX, we operate in a pod model, like very classic pod model for agents.
you.

In terms of content production, where we have like a managing editor and kind of a engagement manager, account manager, working on each account, generating the content, kind of doing the week to week deliverables, making sure that we're on track.
Our director model is actually changing.
Directors used to be over multiple pods of teams that were working on specific accounts.
Directors are now going to be a pooled resource.
So they're rotating across different accounts, across like our full book of business, depending on their strengths.

So when a client might need a technical SEO audit, or might want some help thinking about kind of shifting priorities for being more AEO friendly, like thinking more about AIs and LLMs, we might pull in a director.
So So So
The model is really that there's like an engagement manager who thinks about like the day-to-day relationship.

And then the directors are really like a strategic resource that comes in and supports clients like when they need to do a shift in strategy or we're thinking more about kind of like expanding work streams, things like that.
So that's the way that the directors fit into our operating model today.
This is changing only now.
So kind of any directors that will come in will be operating under this pooled model.
We'll track work across linear tickets.
So thinking about it really like assigning almost engineering resources with a team of experts who have that strategic experience to jump in when needed.
So does that make sense?
Yes.
Yes, it does.
Awesome.
Cool.
Well, let's.
Spend some time talking about you then.
We'd love to kind of get your elevator pitch.
Tell me a little bit about you and your background.
Sure.
So the third version is that, so I finished with my military service, then wandered around for, I don't know, four years, getting jobs here and there, trying to find what I like and whatnot.

Then at some point, I, you know, got on Upwork, started bidding for projects there, did that for a while, got out of Upwork and worked as a freelancer, mainly, you know, for content, content strategy, and so on.
And then in 2020, I decided to launch my own agency, which I did.
At the beginning, the timing, you know, given what happened in the world, like, was not the best, but it ended up working well.
I grew the agency, I didn't speak, it was, we were 18.

Full-time people and contractors working with us, worked with, you know, over 50 B2B SaaS companies, you know, from like PLG, SLG, Enterprise SaaS, Technical SaaS, Marketplaces, Vertical SaaS, and so on and so forth.

So, earlier this year, you know, I got an offer from one of my friends, which was back when I was having a discussion with Marcel, and he, you know, he asked joining him in launching another agency, which we did.
We focused on LinkedIn growth.

And even though, you know, we liked the idea and developed, you know, like very nice processes and like systems around certain things that are in demand right now, such as founder-led content for LinkedIn, the idea didn't quite fly.
And so,

So we are winding it down by the end of the year, and as I, you know, start with Marcel, I'm looking for a change and a new challenge, and this is why, you know, we connected with Marcel again a couple of weeks ago.
Awesome.
Tell me a little bit more about the, like, LinkedIn efforts.
What were you finding was working, what wasn't working?
Would love to, like, hear more about, kind of, your takeaways there, because it's certainly something that a lot of people are interested in, so I could see, like, where the idea came from, you know?
Yeah, sure, Andi.

So the thing is that, you know, one of our mistakes in hindsight was that we started with three different services, which was LinkedIn content, content for, you know, like the SEO's profile, paid, and what we call signal-based outbound, and that was a mistake, because right now the market is, like, there's this,
Cloud of AI and all the volatility in channels that were traditionally reliable, such as Google Search, and people want clarity.
Buyers want clarity.
They don't want you to come in the call and say, these are three services, you know, you can pick one.
No, no, no.
You can just sell clarity by saying that this is what we do, just one thing, right?
And so we understood that a bit late, and then we switched to just what we do is LinkedIn growth, both with content, paid, and outreach.
What worked really well was the content side of things, where we developed processes around, you know, how we should structure interviews with, you know, the CEO, for example, of the company.
And then how you get these interviews, the transcripts, I mean, and, you know, prompts to get ideas.
From ideas, you go to content brief, and then you have the human right, content, and AI to, you know, help in the editing process.
So we got that part right, and I have a situation.

System that works now, but as I said, I guess it didn't pick up as fast as we would like it to pick up, and that's, you know, even though the idea was great, and we were like in love with what we were building, as I said, like it didn't fly, so yeah, but the systems and the methodologies for that part of like the services is there, you know, like, and I got really good exposure, like on how you can build this.

And for what it's worth, I think that it could be a great complimentary service, especially if you're selling to, you know, earlier stage companies to sell them, to cross-sell them, like founder-led content, right?
I don't know if that's something you do already, but just say.

It's like, like, we're seeing a lot of interest in it right now, but as you kind of pointed out, like the tone piece is really, really, really important, especially with like, if you're doing thought leadership.
you're doing thought leadership.
And founder-led content, like having that voice, right?
And actually, everything we produce is actually reversed to what you just said.
So we're generating all the content with AI, and then a human is doing the editorial on the other side.
Yes, yes.
I would say that the tone thing was not the most challenging, because everything that we would put in a piece of content would come directly from the interview.
We would get the prompts I designed were extracting all the segments from the interview that were being used, basically, to create the piece.
So they couldn't say that, yeah, but I didn't say that, or, you know, like, this is not me, or whatnot.
That's what we discussed, you know.
But I also get the, you know, like this reverse process, starting with AI and then using human later in the process.
Yeah.
So talk to me a little bit about your work.
You know, you've now founded two sort of agencies.
So...
How do you think about structuring, like what tools are you using to structure work and to like working with the clients that are in your own book of business or assigning out work to your teams?
Actually, let's start with the first, like if you're managing clients directly, what are you using to prioritize and manage the projects?

I would say mostly, you know, let's say we close a new client, communications usually are on email and from email, as soon as we close them, then, you know, we will send out a form where we request some important, like piece of information to structure the agreements and whatnot.
This is a notion form, and then we will have the kickoff call, in the kickoff call, we have the client collaboration space in Notion, as Marcel did it at the beginning of GrowthX.
I'm not sure if, you know, that still stands.
in Notion, yeah.
Yes.

So the client collaboration space is there, and basically the logic is that you have kind of a master space, and then you have, let's say you have 10 clients you need to serve, you basically mirror what's in the master space in these individual client collaboration spaces, right?
So that they can see progress in terms of the piece of content we are working on, or like the projects we are working on, and so on and so forth.
So communications on Slack, and you use the canvas there to give them access to whatever they need to have access to.
And in terms of drive and deliverables, the main vehicle has been Google Drive for me so far.
Then obviously there are many tools, you know, that we've, and I've used over the years, like for short videos, Looms, and all these SEO tools and whatnot.
But I would say the main ones...
ones...
Our Slack for communications, after we close the client notion, and Google Drive for, you know, like documents and sheets and, and so on.
Yeah.
So, like, in terms of more specifically managing like projects within clients, so, you know, you have the notion space of the things that you're working on.
And, um, right now we have, for example, a couple of clients that are all at the same time asking for like a technical SEO audit and AI strategy.
And we have like one, two people that are really, really good at that.

And then, you know, other people that are more strong on the editorial side thinking about, or thinking about PSEO, but like that strategic, like setting that strategic, um, direction is, is not like their core strength.
Um, so how do you think about like prioritizing across, like asks when everything's coming in at the same time?
And you only have so many hours in a week.
So first of all, as a good marketer, I would say it depends, right?

But there is nuance here because if we are talking about technical SEO, which you mentioned, technical SEO, especially for most B2B SaaS companies, which the website is not as big, it's like a couple of hundred pages, is not going to have a big impact, right?
And I personally don't subscribe to this way of approaching SEO where you go and just say 200 recommendations, let's fix those, and then you go into an endless cycle of technical SEO fixes.
Now, having said that, if we were talking about a bigger website and there is something fundamentally wrong with the website, obviously you should prioritize that, right?
But the way I would prioritize things are, you know, on a case-by-case basis.
See where we will drive the most impact.
And quite frankly, if I think that, you know what, the client may be insisting on something, right?
But I think that there is no reason for us to focus on that or shift our focus from something else over to that.
I would just explain that, try to back my arguments with data.
And yeah.
So talk to me about like how the role you played in driving strategic initiatives for the clients at Minutia.
Like how did you, like once you onboarded a client, were you assigning them out?
Were you like driving the strategic work?
I know you were obviously the founder and the owner.
So lots of like different roles to play.
So how did you think about that?
Yeah, so again, it depends because if you're on board, for example,

For a client with an established content inventory and content program, you have something to work with, meaning you have a content inventory, and somewhere in there, there might be some opportunities for content updates.
These are your first quick wins, right?
And so I would say that, you know what, like, why don't we start doing these content updates, right?
It's important.
And this will give us some, like, momentum, and we will reach time to value faster, which is, as an agency, is exactly what you want, because you have time against you.

In other cases where we have an early stage company, but they found product market fit, we would be like, okay, look, we are not going to do this how-to or this, you know, long shot and, like, big bet initiatives.
Let's just focus on what will drive some impact.
In your case, that might be, let's do the alternative pages, let's do the software listicles, and so on and so forth.
And so, every case is...
And especially if we're talking about B2B SaaS, where I, you know, I've spent most of my time on, it really depends on the maturity of the content program, their stage.
And we come in as a, I don't want to come in as a, just as an execution partner.
I want to come in as a strategic and execution partner so that we have a say on like what we will be working on.
I don't know.
Does that answer your question?
Yeah, yeah.

I would love like an example of where you were like started and, and driving a strategic initiative and like defining the path forward kind of, if you could give me an example and walk me through it, that would be helpful.
Yeah.
So, so we started, that's, that's an interesting one.
So we started working with a business.
This was, this was a business in the promotional products space.
Later they turned into a swag platform, but back then, you know, it was just a.
Promotional products business.
And they were devastated by COVID.
We started working with them back in 2020.
And back then, the intent in the search engine results pages was that you need something like business or company swag ideas, right?
Back then, most of the pieces that were ranking in the top 10, they were listicles, right?
Like, you will see more e-commerce pages in there because Google can monetize those easier, right?
And so we started and we started attacking those one by one.

But soon, after we started, saw that some of them bring kind of the demand that we don't really want to capture, meaning small orders, like I want 10 t-shirts for this whatever party or whatever company event, whereas we were mostly interested in like .
You know, bulk orders for a rebrand of a company with 1,000 employees.

And so we kept doing those, but we positioned the content in a way that was obvious that, you know what, like, if you are going to reach out, contact us, you need to make sure that, like, you are interested in bigger, higher volumes, right?
And as we understood, like, what the audience wants, we kind of shifted towards content that's, or use cases that were mostly focused on, as I said, like, okay, this is going to be a big order.
Like, for example, rebrand related topics and so on, right?
So that's one that comes, you know, that's top of mind.
But there are so many other ones that, like, I could, you know, go on and on and on.
Yeah.
Yeah.
So I'm just taking notes.
Cool.

So can you talk to me about how you divided work across your team and thinking about setting projects in motion, especially like this role to give you some context, this will be something where you will say like, hey, we need to focus more on X or Y, and then the team will go out and execute.
So how do you think about giving the team the right direction, but also space to execute so that you don't get stuck in the weeds?
Yeah, of course.
So at our peak, we had one SEO lead who was leading the client engagements we had, and he had two SEO strategists who were working on SEO-related tasks, mainly content briefing.
I we had two, two, um...
Two to three content leads, and each of them had around four content writers who were, you know, doing the content, and they were leading the, you know, the content creation engagements.
We also had a client success person who was, you know, managing communications and whatnot.
And at some point, we also added an operations person.
In retrospect, overkill, you know, not necessary.
And so that was the kind of the structure of the team.
We also had a designer who was, you know, doing graphics and whatnot when we needed them.
And what I want is the client to feel that there is a team working behind, you know, like your project and like the engagement and whatnot.
So we would have this bi-weekly catch-ups, and we would like the team to be present in those catch-ups.
So that the client feels that, okay, whenever I have an SEO question, there is the SEO lead there to take that question for me.
Or whenever I have a question regarding content pipeline, there is a content lead there to take that question.
Content writers were mostly, and SEO strategists were mostly, you could call them ICs.
They were not involved in client communications and whatnot, even when something was wrong, right?
We would handle it through the SEO lead, the content lead, and so on.

The client success person was the one who was leading the engagement in terms of your communications and whatnot, and also was the one who had to set up the agenda for the call, lead the call on our end, and so on.
They were also the person responsible for cross-sells and upsells, right?
Even though inevitably, most of the time, these were, you know, ended up to me.
And so- Sorry.
Sorry.

Just to redirect you a little bit, I'm really curious about how you like set people up for success from like a training standpoint too, and making sure that like they had all the right context and knew how to execute against like a plan that you were setting or something like that.
So less around org structure and more around like, cause this job is really going to be like an execution role of like setting strategy and working with a team who's trying to execute against a plan.
Yeah.
So the way we did that was that we had, you know, a document where we would go in and just serve all the high level kind of information that the team needs to know about this project.
This is their goal.
This is some context as to why they closed in the first place and so on and so forth.
Okay.
Uh, and we would try, uh, to push towards that direction.
Right.
Um, and make sure that everyone knows when things are off, when things are like in the right direction and on.
And so on.

But I guess to answer your question more directly, we would have this document that we built for each client, and that would detail everything around the engagement and like what is important for this client, some context about the point of contact, and so on and so forth.
And if anyone like from the team, someone from the team had like questions, they could reach out in the internal channel on Slack and just ask.
Okay, cool.
Well, those are all of my questions.
What questions do have for me since we have about five minutes left here?
Sure, so my question is for this particular role, like who are you supposed to report to?
And also, who are you working closely with besides the team that you like described, the managing editor and so on?
it's No.
Is the person you report to like you or, and how does that work in terms of, you know, like making, like being successful in that role?
Yeah, so today the directors report to me, I think that will change.
I know you talked to Kyle previously.
I think Kyle's going to start to own, like supporting the director layer, just because he has more like direct strategic marketing experience.
And I've been, I've been managing to the org structure.
So that was kind of where some of those questions that I asked came from.

The, these are like, I do envision this role as being somebody and I think that like across the org, we want somebody who's going to be like, super, like not need a lot of management, super independent in terms of like, they have.
They have up like, uh, con.

And strategy, they, if somebody files a linear ticket and says help, they know how to like execute, get it off their plate, like it really should be like a ticket system where you're coming in, you're solving problems, and you don't need a lot of like management or support.
Like I think we would like it to be a relatively independent, like role where people can kind of run against the expectations on their own.
Yeah.
And is it, is the work deliverable based?
Like clients expect a certain number of pieces per month or awards per month?
Yeah, we're doing five a week for each client.
And at minimum, and then sometimes there are like multiple work streams, things like that.
Again, you wouldn't be responsible for managing kind of like those week to week deliverables.
This is really about like coming in when clients need a refresh or, or somebody to take a step back.
Okay.
Thank
And do some higher level thinking, And kind of change directions.
Okay.
But, and this is my last question, but like, how many people would you say the account director position role has underneath them?
Like, is it?
Oh, yeah.
So this isn't a management position.
Okay.
Yeah, it was at one point, and that's what we're changing is, like, we want somebody with a strategic mindset.
It's, like, kind of a high-powered individual contributor.
Hmm.
Okay.
That's interesting.
Because, yeah, I think, because I thought that it was kind of like the managing director position or role you had, I don't know, a few months back.
But, yeah, thanks for, yeah.
Yeah, I, we had that.
We found that directors were getting sucked into the weeds in that.
And we want this to be an execution-based role.
And not, like, sort of a management-based role where you're just, like, putting out mini-fires.
Like, we want the directors to be able to, like, have time to do deep work, like, deep thinking.
And if you're, like, splitting time as a manager and kind of doing that strategic execution, there's not room for that, really.
Okay, okay.
That makes sense.
Okay.
All right.
Any other questions?
No, that would be it for now.
Okay.
Awesome.
Well, George, it was great chatting with you.
appreciate your time.
Thank you.
And I know, yeah, you will hear from Tucker, I think, after this.
Thank you very much, Andi.
Have a great day.
Thanks, George.
Bye.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Interviewing George Chasiotis for the Account Director role.](https://fathom.video/share/kb5x8f_iq2ma-NKv1AWebnTG3HTE1iYz?tab=summary&timestamp=154.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Role Shift:** GrowthX is changing its AD model from a management role to a pooled, strategic Individual Contributor (IC) role. This enables deep work by preventing directors from getting "sucked into the weeds" of daily execution.](https://fathom.video/share/kb5x8f_iq2ma-NKv1AWebnTG3HTE1iYz?tab=summary&timestamp=202.0)
  - [**Candidate Profile:** George Chasiotis founded two agencies, Minutia (18 staff, 50+ B2B SaaS clients) and a LinkedIn growth agency (now winding down). He has deep experience in content strategy and operations.](https://fathom.video/share/kb5x8f_iq2ma-NKv1AWebnTG3HTE1iYz?tab=summary&timestamp=299.0)
  - [**Strategic Approach:** Prioritizes high-impact work over client-requested tasks, using data to justify decisions. He focuses on achieving fast time-to-value (TTV) with quick wins, like content updates, to build momentum.](https://fathom.video/share/kb5x8f_iq2ma-NKv1AWebnTG3HTE1iYz?tab=summary&timestamp=892.0)
  - [**Operational Style:** Uses a Notion-centric system for client collaboration and team alignment. He delegates execution to leads (SEO, Content) to maintain a strategic focus, providing direction via a central project document.](https://fathom.video/share/kb5x8f_iq2ma-NKv1AWebnTG3HTE1iYz?tab=summary&timestamp=653.0)

## Topics

### The Account Director Role at GrowthX

  - [**Model:** Shifting from a management role over specific "pods" to a pooled, strategic IC resource.](https://fathom.video/share/kb5x8f_iq2ma-NKv1AWebnTG3HTE1iYz?tab=summary&timestamp=202.0)
  - [**Function:** Directors are called in via Linear tickets for high-level strategic work (e.g., technical SEO audits, AI strategy, shifting priorities).](https://fathom.video/share/kb5x8f_iq2ma-NKv1AWebnTG3HTE1iYz?tab=summary&timestamp=202.0)
  - [**Rationale:** The change enables deep strategic thinking by removing directors from daily management tasks.](https://fathom.video/share/kb5x8f_iq2ma-NKv1AWebnTG3HTE1iYz?tab=summary&timestamp=1664.0)

### Candidate Background & Experience

  - [**Minutia Agency (2020–2023):**](https://fathom.video/share/kb5x8f_iq2ma-NKv1AWebnTG3HTE1iYz?tab=summary&timestamp=339.0)
      - [Grew to 18 full-time staff and contractors.](https://fathom.video/share/kb5x8f_iq2ma-NKv1AWebnTG3HTE1iYz?tab=summary&timestamp=339.0)
      - [Served 50+ B2B SaaS clients (PLG, SLG, Enterprise, Technical, Marketplaces, Vertical).](https://fathom.video/share/kb5x8f_iq2ma-NKv1AWebnTG3HTE1iYz?tab=summary&timestamp=354.0)
  - [**LinkedIn Growth Agency (2023–Present):**](https://fathom.video/share/kb5x8f_iq2ma-NKv1AWebnTG3HTE1iYz?tab=summary&timestamp=382.0)
      - [**Mistake:** Launched with three services (content, paid, signal-based outbound), causing buyer confusion.](https://fathom.video/share/kb5x8f_iq2ma-NKv1AWebnTG3HTE1iYz?tab=summary&timestamp=455.0)
      - [**Correction:** Pivoted to a single, clear "LinkedIn Growth" service.](https://fathom.video/share/kb5x8f_iq2ma-NKv1AWebnTG3HTE1iYz?tab=summary&timestamp=455.0)
      - [**Success:** Developed a scalable process for founder-led content:](https://fathom.video/share/kb5x8f_iq2ma-NKv1AWebnTG3HTE1iYz?tab=summary&timestamp=511.0)
          - [Founder interview → AI-assisted transcript analysis → Content brief → Human-written draft → AI-assisted editing.](https://fathom.video/share/kb5x8f_iq2ma-NKv1AWebnTG3HTE1iYz?tab=summary&timestamp=511.0)
      - [**Outcome:** Winding down due to slow market adoption, but the content system is a proven asset.](https://fathom.video/share/kb5x8f_iq2ma-NKv1AWebnTG3HTE1iYz?tab=summary&timestamp=420.0)

### Strategic Approach & Example

  - [**Prioritization:** Focuses on high-impact work over client-requested tasks. Uses data to justify decisions and push back on low-value requests.](https://fathom.video/share/kb5x8f_iq2ma-NKv1AWebnTG3HTE1iYz?tab=summary&timestamp=832.0)
  - [**Time-to-Value (TTV):** A key goal is achieving fast TTV to build client momentum.](https://fathom.video/share/kb5x8f_iq2ma-NKv1AWebnTG3HTE1iYz?tab=summary&timestamp=955.0)
      - [**Established Clients:** Start with quick wins like content updates.](https://fathom.video/share/kb5x8f_iq2ma-NKv1AWebnTG3HTE1iYz?tab=summary&timestamp=955.0)
      - [**Early-Stage Clients:** Focus on high-impact pages (e.g., alternatives, software listicles).](https://fathom.video/share/kb5x8f_iq2ma-NKv1AWebnTG3HTE1iYz?tab=summary&timestamp=993.0)
  - [**Case Study: Promotional Products Client (2020)**](https://fathom.video/share/kb5x8f_iq2ma-NKv1AWebnTG3HTE1iYz?tab=summary&timestamp=1049.0)
      - [**Problem:** Initial content attracted low-value, small-volume orders.](https://fathom.video/share/kb5x8f_iq2ma-NKv1AWebnTG3HTE1iYz?tab=summary&timestamp=1064.0)
      - [**Solution:** Shifted strategy to target high-value use cases (e.g., company rebrands) and explicitly positioned content for bulk orders.](https://fathom.video/share/kb5x8f_iq2ma-NKv1AWebnTG3HTE1iYz?tab=summary&timestamp=1064.0)

### Team Management & Operations

  - [**Structure:** At Minutia, the team was organized into leads (SEO, Content) who managed ICs (strategists, writers).](https://fathom.video/share/kb5x8f_iq2ma-NKv1AWebnTG3HTE1iYz?tab=summary&timestamp=1236.0)
  - [**Client Communication:** Leads, not ICs, handled client communication to maintain a consistent strategic voice.](https://fathom.video/share/kb5x8f_iq2ma-NKv1AWebnTG3HTE1iYz?tab=summary&timestamp=1332.0)
  - [**Delegation:** Provided direction via a central Notion document detailing project goals and context. This gave leads autonomy for execution.](https://fathom.video/share/kb5x8f_iq2ma-NKv1AWebnTG3HTE1iYz?tab=summary&timestamp=1380.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**Tucker:** Follow up with George Chasiotis after the interview.](https://fathom.video/share/kb5x8f_iq2ma-NKv1AWebnTG3HTE1iYz?tab=summary&timestamp=1721.0)
